assessments.whatChanged: The baseline (docs/REVIEW-2026-06-09.md:13,59-63) judged the node web tier unfit for production traffic: "one malformed JSON POST crashes the whole server" — server.ts did `void handle(...)` with no catch, parseBody called raw JSON.parse, no global rejection handler, no body cap, sockets hung with no 500. Three committed hardening passes (6e44672 "harden the web tier", 621fe4c "resolve all 29 REVIEW findings", 2c236da "edge hardening parity + streaming render deadline") closed essentially all of it. The work is COMMITTED, not in-flight; the tree is clean for packages/runtime. packages/runtime/src/server.ts is now a fully hardened node:http server, packages/runtime/src/request.ts wraps JSON.parse, and packages/cloudflare/src/fetch-handler.ts brings the edge adapter to parity (the prior CVE-review P0 about the CF adapter bypassing all hardening).
assessments.summary: The node web tier is now production-grade for an unauthenticated client-facing deploy: per-request error boundary that never throws, 1 MiB body cap with early socket-read abort, async handler timeout (503), socket-level slow-loris/slow-body/header-size limits, process-level unhandledRejection net, graceful SIGTERM drain wired end-to-end, and a client-disconnect AbortSignal. 75 runtime + 22 cloudflare tests pass green. The remaining gaps are honest and bounded: (1) the Cloudflare edge adapter has the error boundary + JSON safety + security headers but NO app-level body cap and NO handler timeout — it leans entirely on the Cloudflare platform (100 MB / CPU limits), which is defensible for that target but is not parity with node; (2) the handler timeout is explicitly an async-only deadline and does NOT defend against event-loop-blocking sync work (ReDoS, while(true), huge sync JSON.parse) — documented as out of scope at server.ts:96-102; (3) uncaughtException is deliberately not netted (correct per Node guidance). Score 8/10: a node deploy behind a proxy is safe to expose; docking points are the edge body-cap gap and the inherent sync-blocking DoS class.

items.name: Per-request error boundary (no throw escapes to process)
items.evidence: packages/runtime/src/server.ts:756-848 handle() wraps the whole request in try/catch/finally; server.ts:598-599 `void handle(...)` comment 'handle swallows every throw internally and always answers the socket'. Tested: server.test.ts catches a throwing handler -> 500.
items.note: Was the baseline's #1 crash vector; now fully closed and access-logged once per request.

items.name: Malformed-JSON body no longer crashes (SyntaxError -> 400)
items.evidence: packages/runtime/src/request.ts:88-99 parseBody try/catch JSON.parse -> RuntimeError('RUNTIME_INVALID_JSON') mapped to 400; edge mirror at packages/cloudflare/src/fetch-handler.ts:122-127 returns {ok:false} -> 400.
items.note: The exact DoS the baseline called out (request.ts:86) is fixed on both node and edge.

items.name: Request body-size cap (memory-exhaustion)
items.evidence: packages/runtime/src/server.ts:293-337 readBody tallies bytes, rejects at >maxBytes (default 1 MiB, DEFAULT_MAX_BODY_BYTES line 213), drops buffered chunks and ignores later chunks so the heap stays bounded; server.ts:316 RUNTIME_BODY_TOO_LARGE -> 413. Tested at server.test.ts:260 'refuses an oversized body with 413 and reads no more of it'.
items.note: Node only. The edge adapter (decodeBody, fetch-handler.ts:115-131) does request.text() with NO cap — see edge gap below.

items.name: Handler/request timeout (hung or slow async controller)
items.evidence: packages/runtime/src/server.ts:348-371 withTimeout races app.handle against handlerTimeoutMs (default 30s, line 216) -> RUNTIME_HANDLER_TIMEOUT -> 503; applied at server.ts:805-812. Tested server.test.ts:455 'answers 503 when a handler overruns its timeout, and keeps serving'. Timer is unref'd; late settlement is swallowed (no unhandled rejection).
items.note: Honestly documented (server.ts:96-102) as an async-only deadline: it cannot interrupt event-loop-blocking sync work, only release the socket on slow I/O.

items.name: Socket-level slow-loris / slow-body / header-size limits
items.evidence: packages/runtime/src/server.ts:487-494 applyServerLimits sets requestTimeout(30s)/headersTimeout(15s)/keepAliveTimeout(5s); maxHeaderSize 16 KiB set at createServer (server.ts:595, DEFAULT_MAX_HEADER_BYTES line 220). Tuned below Node defaults for a public tier. Test 'accepts tuned socket limits and serves normally'.
items.note: Covers slow-loris, slow-body, and oversized-header out of the box. Node only (edge is on Cloudflare's own limits).

items.name: Process-level rejection net (defense-in-depth)
items.evidence: packages/runtime/src/server.ts:929-942 installProcessSafetyNet registers a single idempotent unhandledRejection listener that logs and keeps serving; installed at serve() line 592. Deliberately does NOT net uncaughtException (server.ts:920-924, correct per Node guidance). Tested server.test.ts:746.
items.note: Idempotent via WeakSet so booting many servers in one process registers one listener.

items.name: Graceful drain on SIGTERM for the web tier
items.evidence: packages/runtime/src/server.ts:534-556 drainServer (stop accepting, closeIdleConnections, force closeAllConnections after graceMs default 10s); Server.close() at server.ts:617 calls it; wired through packages/cli/src/run.ts:143,320 installShutdown for BOTH serve and dev; packages/cli/src/bin.ts:65-72 binds SIGTERM/SIGINT -> drain().then(exit 0). Tests server.test.ts:1068 cover both finish-before-grace and force-after-grace.
items.note: Full chain verified end-to-end: rolling restarts will not drop in-flight requests or hang forever.

items.name: Client-disconnect cancellation (no work for a dead socket)
items.evidence: packages/runtime/src/server.ts:727-742 requestAbortSignal fires on res 'close' when !writableFinished -> RUNTIME_CLIENT_DISCONNECTED; published on context.signal at server.ts:775. Edge uses request.signal (fetch-handler.ts:163).
items.note: Lets streaming SSR / long handlers stop rendering into a hung-up client.

items.name: Edge (Cloudflare) adapter: NO app-level body cap or handler timeout
items.evidence: packages/cloudflare/src/fetch-handler.ts:115-131 decodeBody does `await request.text()` with no byte tally and no limit; grep for maxBody/413/content-length/timeout in fetch-handler.ts returns nothing. The error boundary, JSON-parse safety, and security headers ARE at parity (fetch-handler.ts:184-220, 255).
items.note: Defensible (Cloudflare caps body ~100MB and CPU time per request), but not parity with node's 1 MiB cap. Add an app-level content-length / streamed-byte cap -> 413 before request.text() for defense-in-depth and a tighter app-chosen limit.

items.name: Event-loop-blocking synchronous DoS (ReDoS, while(true), huge sync parse)
items.evidence: packages/runtime/src/server.ts:96-102 documents that handlerTimeoutMs is a setTimeout and 'is NOT a defense against event-loop-blocking synchronous work ... which starves the timer and every other request alike.'
items.note: Inherent to single-threaded JS; mitigated by 'not writing it' (router refuses ambiguous backtracking patterns) rather than a runtime guard. Real but architectural; not a quick fix. The body cap blunts the huge-sync-JSON.parse variant.

assessments.dimension: security primitives AND whether they are WIRED
assessments.whatChanged: A real middleware pipeline now exists and the four BEFORE security bugs are all fixed. `packages/web/src/middleware.ts` adds `runPipeline` (onion fold), `Application` threads `middleware` through every dispatch (application.ts:92), and `packages/kernel/src/secure-stack.ts` composes cors/ratelimit/originCheck/csrf in safe order — turning the previously "unwired IOU" libraries into something mountable. CORS wildcard+credentials now throws `CORS_WILDCARD_WITH_CREDENTIALS` (cors.ts:118). CSRF tokens are session-bound via HMAC over nonce+sessionId (csrf/token.ts:34-37). Webhook secrets are stored as a `secretId` reference resolved at delivery, never persisted (webhooks.ts:113,331), and a DNS-rebinding-aware SSRF guard blocks private/reserved/metadata ranges (webhooks.ts:207-247). The Cloudflare edge adapter now runs the SAME `@lesto/web` hardening as the node server — context, error boundary, security headers (fetch-handler.ts:231-262) — closing the prior P0 edge-bypass. Session cookie is `__Host-`+Secure+HttpOnly+SameSite=Lax (identity/cookies.ts:21-37).

items.name: Middleware pipeline exists and runs on every dispatch
items.evidence: packages/web/src/application.ts:92 `const response = await runPipeline(this.middleware, request, () => this.dispatch(...))`; packages/web/src/middleware.ts:52 reduceRight onion fold
items.note: The core gap from the BEFORE review ('aspirational IOUs unwired') is closed at the framework level. Empty list == byte-for-byte legacy behavior.

items.name: secureStack composes cors/ratelimit/originCheck/csrf in safe order
items.evidence: packages/kernel/src/secure-stack.ts:85-109 builds list cors -> rateLimit -> originCheck -> csrf, each conditional on options presence
items.note: Ordering is correct (CORS outermost so error responses carry headers; rate-limit before crypto).

items.name: CORS wildcard + credentials guard
items.evidence: packages/cors/src/cors.ts:118-124 throws CORS_WILDCARD_WITH_CREDENTIALS; allow-list echoes only members (resolveOrigin:85-98) and adds Vary: Origin when echoed
items.note: Fully fixes the BEFORE bug (reflect-any-origin + Allow-Credentials:true).

items.name: CSRF token session binding
items.evidence: packages/csrf/src/token.ts:34-37 sign = HMAC(nonce + \0 + sessionId, secret); verifyToken recomputes for presenting session (token.ts:66) with constant-time + length guard
items.note: A token minted for session A no longer verifies under B. Closes the BEFORE lateral-replay gap.

items.name: Origin / Fetch-Metadata CSRF defense (zero-token)
items.evidence: packages/csrf/src/origin.ts:84-113 reads Sec-Fetch-Site then Origin (never Host), fail-closed, never reads Content-Type (immune to the SvelteKit/Astro/Hono content-type-bypass CVE class)
items.note: Strong design; the cheap recommended default. Estate uses it.

items.name: Webhook HMAC secret at rest + SSRF guard
items.evidence: packages/webhooks/src/webhooks.ts:113 persists only secretId; :207-247 defaultUrlGuard blocks loopback/RFC1918/link-local(169.254 metadata)/CGNAT/IPv6 ULA + canonicalizes IPv6 against uncompressed-spelling evasion; blocks on ANY private resolved IP (rebinding)
items.note: Both BEFORE webhook bugs fixed and the SSRF guard is unusually thorough.

items.name: Cloudflare edge adapter hardening parity
items.evidence: packages/cloudflare/src/fetch-handler.ts:231-262 runWithContext + dispatchHardened (error boundary, statusForError) + withSecurityHeaders; uses cf-connecting-ip not forgeable headers (:166)
items.note: Resolves prior P0 'edge is a weaker second front door'. secureStack middleware runs on the edge too since it lives inside app.handle.

items.name: Secure / __Host- session cookies
items.evidence: packages/identity/src/cookies.ts:21 SESSION_COOKIE = '__Host-lesto_session'; :31 Path=/; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax
items.note: Correct cookie hardening with documented reverse-proxy and localhost-dev caveats.

items.name: Security middleware actually mounted in a default deploy
items.evidence: Only examples/estate/src/app.ts:38 `middleware: secureStack({ originCheck: {} })` — no cors, no rateLimit, no csrf token. examples/blog/src/app.ts:24 createApp with NO middleware; create-lesto scaffold (templates.ts) ships none. AppConfig.middleware default is absent (kernel.ts:69).
items.note: The batteries exist but a fresh `lesto` app has zero CORS/rate-limit/CSRF. Default deploy posture is still unprotected. Wire a sane secureStack default into the scaffold + blog.

items.name: RBAC wired into request handling
items.evidence: grep '@lesto/rbac' across packages/examples returns only packages/rbac/src/index.ts — no controller, middleware, kernel, or example imports Permissions/can(). It is pure logic with no enforcement seam.
items.note: Authorization is a library no request flows through. There is no authorize() middleware nor controller integration.

items.name: Session store production substrate
items.evidence: packages/auth/src/sessions.ts:7 only MemorySessionStore ships; comment 'default substrate for tests and dev'. No SQL/Redis SessionStore in tree.
items.note: Interface is clean (SessionStore) but the only impl is in-process; sessions evaporate on restart and can't span a fleet. Same for MemoryRateLimitStore (ratelimit/store.ts:10).

items.name: Rate-limit keying trustworthy under proxies
items.evidence: packages/ratelimit/src/middleware.ts:13 keys off currentContext() ip; :26 missing IP falls back to one shared bucket (tightens, not skips). runtime/trust-proxy.ts defaults to trust-nothing (socket peer), opt-in hops.
items.note: Correct: forwarded headers are not believed unless trustProxy is configured; absent IP fails tight.

assessments.summary: Major, genuine progress since the BEFORE baseline. The central risk that review called out — security primitives as isolated, unmountable libraries — is largely resolved: there is now a real onion middleware pipeline (web/middleware.ts + Application) and a `secureStack` composition (kernel/secure-stack.ts), and the four specific security bugs (CORS wildcard+credentials, CSRF session binding, webhook plaintext-secret, webhook SSRF) are all fixed in the current tree with strong implementations. The Cloudflare edge adapter now shares the node hardening, closing the prior P0. Cookies are correctly `__Host-`/Secure/HttpOnly/SameSite. What holds the score at 6 rather than higher: the pipeline is opt-in and almost nobody opts in. A default scaffolded Lesto app and examples/blog mount NO security middleware at all; examples/estate mounts only originCheck (no CORS, rate-limit, or CSRF token). So the primitives are now wirable and correct, but the default-deploy posture is still unprotected. RBAC is pure logic wired into zero request paths — there is no authorize() seam. And the only shipped session/rate-limit stores are in-memory, so neither survives a restart or scales across a fleet. The quickest high-value win is wiring a sane secureStack default into the create-lesto scaffold (low-hanging fruit, S).

assessments.whatChanged: Since the previous run (run2, 5.5) the data layer itself is essentially UNCHANGED in the working tree — `git status`/`git diff` show zero modifications to packages/{orm,migrate,queue,db,runtime}. The in-flight uncommitted churn (kernel.ts, identity, mcp, examples/blog) does not touch the data layer. The recent COMMITTED work continued ADR 0004: `@lesto/orm` is now explicitly marked legacy (commit db41e83) and the new `@lesto/db` Drizzle-shaped typed query DSL (commits df1beaa, 5c9d6d0, plus Phase C migrations of identity/mailing-lists/admin/blog onto it) is now the primary data layer. `@lesto/db` is well-built for safety: every value rides a `?`/named placeholder, identifiers are quoted via quoteIdentifier (db/src/identifier.ts:18) with NUL rejection, orderBy columns come from the typed Column object (queries.ts:158 `column.spec.name`, not raw strings), and UPDATE/DELETE structurally REQUIRE a WHERE (queries.ts:303,362). Tests grew to 120 passing across the four packages (was 78). The architectural ceiling is identical: still 100% synchronous, still no Postgres adapter.
assessments.summary: Safety at the single-process SQLite level is genuinely good and the new @lesto/db layer raises code quality further: parameterized values, quoted identifiers with NUL rejection, mandatory WHERE on update/delete, per-migration BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK transactionality with correct rollback-refusal on missing definitions, and a real queue worker error boundary that survives transient DB faults (report+backoff+continue, with a throwing-reporter guard). 120 tests pass. BUT the dimension's most important question is unmoved: SqlDatabase/SqlStatement remain fully SYNCHRONOUS in ALL FOUR surfaces (orm/types.ts:14-22, db/sql.ts:16-25, queue/types.ts:76-85, migrate/types.ts), and there is still NO Postgres adapter — better-sqlite3 / bun:sqlite (runtime/src/sqlite.ts) are the only concrete drivers. The pervasive "a Postgres driver will satisfy the same shape tomorrow" comments are aspirational and false for a real networked driver: a sync prepare/run/get/all surface cannot back an async Postgres pool without a breaking rewrite. orm/connection.ts still uses a module-global active connection (no pool, no per-request scoping). This single-process sync architecture caps the entire stateless-horizontal-scale story regardless of query-builder polish. Net: production-ready for a single-instance SQLite deployment; not for a horizontally-scaled fleet. Holds at 5.5.

items.name: SqlDatabase / SqlStatement still SYNCHRONOUS (the scale cap)
items.evidence: packages/db/src/sql.ts:16-25 `run(params?): { changes; lastInsertRowid }`, `get(params?): unknown`, `all(params?): unknown[]` — all return values, not Promises; `prepare(sql)` is sync. Same sync shape in packages/orm/src/types.ts:14-22, packages/queue/src/types.ts:76-85, packages/migrate/src/types.ts. All callers are sync: queries.ts:170 `sql.prepare(stmt).get(params)`, queue.ts:183-365 no await on db ops.
items.note: A networked async Postgres pool cannot satisfy this interface without a breaking rewrite of every call site across orm/db/queue/migrate. This is THE structural ceiling on stateless horizontal scale.

items.name: No Postgres adapter (only driver is SQLite)
items.evidence: grep for postgres/pg/pglite in packages/*/src yields only doc comments + unrelated content-* hits. The only concrete drivers are better-sqlite3 and bun:sqlite at packages/runtime/src/sqlite-drivers.ts:19,42, adapted in sqlite.ts:66-87. kernel.ts:34, migrate/types.ts:5, queue/types.ts:5 all say 'future Postgres' — none exists.
items.note: Blocked behind the sync-interface rewrite above. Caps deployment to single-instance SQLite.

items.name: ORM/db identifier injection (quoting + allowlist)
items.evidence: packages/db/src/identifier.ts:18-26 quoteIdentifier doubles embedded quotes + rejects NUL; conditions.ts:38 binds values via `?`, never interpolates; queries.ts:116,127,259,330 route every identifier through quoteIdentifier; orderBy column is the typed Column object (queries.ts:158 `column.spec.name`), not a raw string. Legacy orm/src/identifier.ts:46 quoteColumn adds an optional column allowlist (ORM_UNKNOWN_COLUMN).
items.note: Structurally sound on both the legacy orm and the new @lesto/db layer. Injection is closed.

items.name: Migrations transactional (partial-failure safety)
items.evidence: packages/migrate/src/migrator.ts:80-101 wraps EACH migration in db.exec('BEGIN') / 'COMMIT' with a best-effort ROLLBACK in catch that does not mask the original error; rollback() refuses a missing-definition migration with MIGRATE_MISSING_MIGRATION (migrator.ts:129-135) rather than reversing the wrong one.
items.note: Per-migration atomicity matches Rails semantics. DB is source of truth for applied set. Correct.

items.name: Queue worker error boundary
items.evidence: packages/queue/src/queue.ts:308-320 loop() wraps runOnce in try/catch, reports via report() then sleeps pollMs and continues — a transient claim/reclaim fault no longer kills the worker. report() (284-301) guards against a throwing onError reporter. Handler throws route through fail() with exponential backoff (fail():368-400, backoffMs:402).
items.note: At-least-once contract holds: a job claimed before a throw stays running until visibility deadline, then reclaim returns it.

items.name: Connection management (pool / per-request scoping)
items.evidence: packages/orm/src/connection.ts module-global active connection returned for all models — no pool, no AsyncLocalStorage scoping. @lesto/db's createDb (queries.ts:398) takes one injected handle, so scoping is the consumer's problem; no pooling primitive exists.
items.note: Acceptable for single-process SQLite; insufficient for a connection-pooled networked DB. Tied to the Postgres gap.

assessments.whatChanged: Since the REVIEW-2026-06-09 baseline, the only ops/observability-relevant change is the tracer idempotency fix (commit 621fe4c "resolve all 29 confirmed REVIEW findings"): tracer.ts:56-67 now guards re-export with `if (this.ended) return;`, fixing the baseline "tracer end() not idempotent -> double-export (observability/tracer.ts:53)" finding. The current uncommitted in-flight churn (bun.lock, db/identity/mcp/kernel test files) does NOT touch packages/observability, packages/deploy, or packages/runtime (git diff --stat HEAD on those three is empty), so operability is essentially unchanged from the last commit. No new tracing, metrics, or deploy targets were added.

items.name: @lesto/observability imported by any other package
items.evidence: `grep -rl @lesto/observability packages --include=*.ts | grep -v packages/observability` returns nothing; same for package.json deps. The package is a standalone tracing core (index.ts:1-30) with zero consumers in the monorepo.
items.note: It is a well-built, fully-tested (observability.test.ts) OTel-flavored span/tracer, but completely orphaned. Nothing in the request path mints a span. Dead capability until wired into runtime/kernel/db.

items.name: Tracer end() idempotency (baseline bug)
items.evidence: tracer.ts:60 `if (this.ended) return;` then sets `this.ended = true` before `this.exporter.export(...)` at :66 — second end() is a no-op. Fixed in commit 621fe4c.
items.note: The one REVIEW finding in this dimension; genuinely fixed.

items.name: Span export adapter (OTel / real backend)
items.evidence: index.ts:20 exports only `InMemoryExporter`; exporter.ts:7-13 is `spans.push(span)` into an array. JSDoc at index.ts:17 admits 'an OTel exporter is a future adapter.'
items.note: No way to get spans off the box. Even if wired, traces go to an in-memory array, not Honeycomb/OTLP/Datadog.

items.name: Request access logging (Node runtime)
items.evidence: server.ts:845 `deps.logRequest({ method, path, status, ms: deps.now()-start, requestId })` in the finally block; defaultLogRequest at :903-905 emits `method path status ms requestId`. AccessEntry (:58-74) carries a per-request correlation id minted at :768.
items.note: Solid per-request access log with latency + correlation id on the Node tier. This is the strongest ops feature present.

items.name: Request logging on the Cloudflare edge (real prod target)
items.evidence: fetch-handler.ts:242-243 wires `logError` (errors only) but there is NO logRequest/access-log equivalent in the edge handler (toFetchHandler, :231-260). Per memory, the CF adapter is the actual deploy target.
items.note: The Node server logs every request; the edge target logs only errors. On the real prod runtime you'd be blind to latency/status distribution. Mirroring the AccessEntry log into the fetch handler is a small, high-value fix.

items.name: Traces/metrics in the request path
items.evidence: `grep -rni 'log|trace|metric|span' packages/kernel/src` returns nothing; kernel.ts (112 lines) has no instrumentation. The request handler (server.ts:760-848) emits an access line but starts no span and records no metric.
items.note: No latency histograms, no error-rate counters, no distributed traces. Only the one-line access log. You can grep logs but cannot graph SLOs.

items.name: Health: liveness + readiness endpoints
items.evidence: healthResponse (server.ts:381-403): `/health` -> bare 200 'ok'; `/readyz` -> consults `options.isReady`, 503 'not ready' when false. On by default (:579 `options.health ?? {}`). Probed before app dispatch (:798-801).
items.note: Proper liveness/readiness split with HEAD support and pass-through to real routes — good shape for k8s/LB probes.

items.name: Readiness probe actually checks dependencies
items.evidence: isReady defaults to `() => true` (server.ts:395). `grep -rn isReady` shows NO caller passes a real probe — CLI runServe (run.ts:134-147) calls `deps.serve(app, { port })` with no health options, and bin.ts:57 passes the raw `serve`. So /readyz always returns 200 even with a dead DB.
items.note: The readiness endpoint exists but is hollow — never wired to DB connectivity. A load balancer would route traffic to a node whose DB is down. Wiring isReady to a db ping is a small fix with real safety value.

items.name: lesto deploy: real target vs file-copy + routing printer
items.evidence: runDeploy (run.ts:344-379): builds static sites, planDeploy (plan.ts:217-232) emits per-site StaticTarget/NodeTarget + RoutingRules, shipStatic (ship.ts:55-71) loops files through an uploader. Default uploader nodeUploader (ship.ts:82-105) is a filesystem copy into distDir. Node/dynamic target just prints `run \`lesto serve\` (dynamic)` (run.ts:370). Routing rules are printed to stdout (run.ts:374-375).
items.note: Honest 'ship to a local directory' default + a clean injectable uploader seam (the shape a CDN/S3 backend would take), but no real backend ships in-tree. The dynamic tier is not deployed at all — it just prints a command. It's a planner + local file copy, not a deploy-to-cloud.

items.name: Deploy safety: health gate / rollback / canary / atomic swap
items.evidence: `grep -rni 'rollback|canary|blue-green|atomic|symlink' packages/deploy/src packages/cli/src` returns nothing. shipStatic writes files in-place; no versioned dir, no symlink swap, no post-deploy health check, no rollback path.
items.note: A deploy overwrites live files with no atomicity, no verification, and no way back. Unsafe for prod even against the local target.

items.name: Graceful drain / rolling-restart support
items.evidence: drainServer (server.ts:534-552) stops accepting then waits for in-flight with a drainTimeoutMs grace window; bin.ts:64-72 installs SIGTERM/SIGINT -> `drain().then(() => process.exit(0))`. installProcessSafetyNet (:907+) catches stray unhandledRejection and keeps serving.
items.note: Genuinely good: graceful shutdown with timeout + signal wiring + a process-level safety net. This is the most production-shaped part of the dimension.

assessments.summary: Operability is a tale of two halves. The Node runtime tier is surprisingly mature: per-request access logging with a correlation id (server.ts:845), liveness/readiness endpoints (server.ts:381-403), graceful drain on SIGTERM/SIGINT with a timeout (server.ts:534, bin.ts:64-72), and a process-level safety net. The baseline tracer double-export bug is fixed (tracer.ts:60). But the actual observability story is thin and the deploy story is a planner, not a deployer. The @lesto/observability tracing core is fully orphaned — zero importers, in-memory-only exporter, no OTel adapter — so there are no traces or metrics in any request path; you get one log line per request and nothing graphable. The readiness probe is hollow (isReady defaults to () => true and no caller wires it to the DB, so /readyz lies). The real prod target — the Cloudflare edge handler — logs errors only, no access log. `lesto deploy` builds static sites, copies files to a local dist dir via an injectable uploader, and prints routing rules; the dynamic tier is never deployed (it prints `lesto serve`), and there is no atomic swap, health gate, canary, or rollback. Could you run this in prod and SEE it? Partially — you'd have request logs and health endpoints on the Node tier, but no metrics, no traces, a lying readiness probe, and no access logs on the edge. Could you deploy safely? No — the deploy path is a non-atomic file copy with no verification or rollback. Net: 4/10, up 1 from baseline on the tracer fix; the request-path/health/drain foundations are real but the metrics, edge logging, readiness wiring, and actual deploy/rollback are the missing operability layer.

assessments.whatChanged: Since run2 (maturity-ci=6, dominant OPEN gap = "coverage never gated in CI"), that gap is now CLOSED. scripts/coverage-gate.ts (a deliberately SERIAL gate, well-documented at lines 1-19) is wired into CI as `ws:test:cov` at .github/workflows/ci.yml:45-46 and runs green: 40 non-content packages declare 100% lines/branches/functions/statements thresholds (e.g. packages/queue/vitest.config.ts) and the gate exits 0. The "100% bar" is no longer aspirational on PRs. ws:test => 2952 passed / 0 failed (up from 2855 in run2, 2025 in original review); ws:test exit 0. Format debt is gone: ws:format:check now exits 0 with every package — including all 15 content-* — reporting "All matched files use the correct format" (run2's content-* format debt is paid down). Legacy CommonJS root stays deleted (ls lib/ bin/ test/ => No such file or directory; no "tracks" in package.json — root is private:true workspace-only). Every former zero-test content package retains real suites (content-mcp 80 tests, content-core 688, etc.); only @lesto/e2e is testless by design (separate Playwright CI job, ci.yml:69-89). HEAD has advanced past the stale git-status snapshot to a93311e (ADR 0004 Phase D + app-assembly refactor), tree otherwise clean.

items.name: Coverage gating in CI (was the run2 top gap)
items.evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:45-46 `Test (coverage-gated) / run: bun run ws:test:cov`; scripts/coverage-gate.ts:39 spawns `bun run test:cov` per package; ran `bun run ws:test:cov` => 'Coverage gate passed.' exit 0; 40 non-content packages carry test:cov + 100% thresholds (packages/queue/vitest.config.ts thresholds lines/functions/branches/statements:100)
items.note: The single biggest maturity win since run2. Serial-by-design to avoid v8+--filter CPU oversubscription flaking coverage %.

items.name: CI gates typecheck + lint + test (blocking)
items.evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:33-58 — Typecheck (ws:typecheck), Lint (oxlint), Test (coverage-gated), content tests, integration tests all blocking; separate e2e job ci.yml:69-89 with Playwright chromium; concurrency cancel-in-progress ci.yml:15-17
items.note: Well-architected single 'check' job + dedicated e2e job.

items.name: ws:test full suite green
items.evidence: `bun run ws:test` => total 2952 tests passed, exit 0 (run2: 2855; original review: 2025). MDX '✘ ERROR' lines are expected output of a negative test (broken.mdx error-handling case), not failures.
items.note: +97 tests since run2.

items.name: Format check now green everywhere (content-* debt paid)
items.evidence: `bun run ws:format:check` exit 0; every package incl. content-core (77 files), content-embeddings, content-shared reports 'All matched files use the correct format.'
items.note: run2 listed content-* format debt as the reason the CI format step is informational; the debt itself is now gone.

items.name: CI format step still continue-on-error despite green
items.evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:62-64 `Format check (informational) ... continue-on-error: true` — yet ws:format:check exits 0 across all packages
items.note: One-line flip (delete continue-on-error) makes format blocking now that debt is paid. Pure hygiene fruit.

items.name: Legacy CommonJS root stays deleted
items.evidence: `ls lib bin test` => 'No such file or directory'; `grep -i tracks package.json` => empty; root package.json is private:true, workspaces:[packages/*], no name/main/test
items.note: Carried green from run2.

items.name: Stray non-bun lockfiles tracked (toolchain hygiene)
items.evidence: `git ls-files | grep lock` => package-lock.json (root, stale Jun 7), loom/package-lock.json, packages/content-markdown/pnpm-lock.yaml, packages/content-shared/pnpm-lock.yaml — against a bun@1.3.5-only toolchain (packageManager in package.json). 2 package-lock.json tracked.
items.note: Same 4 stray lockfiles flagged in run2 — unmoved. `git rm` them; .gitignore already exists. Cheap win.

items.name: content-* suites excluded from coverage gate (ratcheted, not gated)
items.evidence: scripts/coverage-gate.ts:27 `if (dir.startsWith('content-')) continue;` skips all 15 content-* packages; they have test:cov scripts but no enforced thresholds (CONTENT_COVERAGE.md ratchet policy). ci.yml:51-52 runs them ungated.
items.note: Transparent and documented; tests run on every PR but %-coverage isn't enforced for these 15 packages.

items.name: Bus factor / version maturity / release automation
items.evidence: `git rev-list --count HEAD` => 43, all commits dated 2026-06-09/10; first commit 2026-06-09 09:10; all package versions 0.0.0 (queue/kernel/db/identity); no git tags, nothing published, no changesets/dependabot/release workflow in .github/
items.note: Single-author, ~1.5-day-old prototype. Structural; only matures with time + a second contributor + a publish pipeline.

assessments.summary: Release-engineering maturity moved from ~6 to ~7 since run2, driven by closing the single dominant gap that capped the prior score: coverage is now actually GATED in CI. scripts/coverage-gate.ts (a deliberately serial, well-reasoned gate) runs via ws:test:cov as a blocking CI step and passes, enforcing 100% lines/branches/functions/statements across 40 framework packages — the 'non-negotiable 100% bar' is real on PRs now, not aspirational. CI is well-architected: typecheck, lint, and the coverage-gated test run all block on every push and PR, with a dedicated Playwright e2e job and concurrency cancel-in-progress. ws:test is 2952 green / 0 fail (up from 2855), the legacy CommonJS root stays deleted, every former zero-test content package has real suites, and the content-* formatting debt that justified the informational format step is fully paid — ws:format:check now exits 0 everywhere. Remaining gaps are mostly cheap hygiene plus one structural ceiling: the CI format step is still continue-on-error even though format is green (one-line flip to blocking); 4 stray non-bun lockfiles (root + loom package-lock.json, 2 content-* pnpm-lock.yaml) are still tracked against a bun-only toolchain; the 15 content-* suites run but are excluded from the coverage gate by design; and the unmovable ceiling is that this is a single-author, ~1.5-day-old, untagged, unpublished prototype with every package at 0.0.0 and no changesets/dependabot/release automation. Cheap wins left: flip the format step to blocking and `git rm` the 4 stray lockfiles.

assessments.whatChanged: The in-flight working-tree changes from the snapshot are now committed (HEAD a93311e / db41e83); git diff is clean. Across the eight flagged correctness issues, seven are fully resolved in the current tree with guards and tests, and one (ui treeJsonSchema children allow-list) remains a known, intentional partial. ADR-0004 migrations (create-lesto now scaffolds @lesto/db with `latest` deps, not workspace:*) and the content-core getCollections()/paginate hardening landed. All target package suites pass (i18n 18, observability 12, ui 29, ui-kit 142).
assessments.summary: Framework correctness is in strong shape. Seven of eight medium correctness issues are genuinely fixed with defensive guards plus regression tests, all verified green. The lone residual is a deliberate, documented looseness: treeJsonSchema does not restrict an allow-list policy's children to the allowed types (it emits any-node), so the model-facing schema is looser than validateTree enforces — a child the schema admits can still be rejected at validation. That is a polish gap, not a safety hole (the validator still bites). Overall correctness scores 8.5/10.

items.name: i18n prototype-member lookup (packages/i18n)
items.evidence: packages/i18n/src/i18n.ts:34 `return Object.hasOwn(catalog, key) ? catalog[key] : undefined;` (own() guards both locale and fallback reads via lookup() at :93/:99); packages/i18n/src/interpolate.ts:17 `const value = Object.hasOwn(params, name) ? params[name] : undefined;`
items.note: A key like "constructor" no longer resolves an inherited Object.prototype member; both catalog and param reads are own-property guarded. 18 i18n tests pass.

items.name: tracer double-export idempotency (packages/observability/src/tracer.ts)
items.evidence: packages/observability/src/tracer.ts:60 `if (this.ended) return;` then :62 `this.ended = true;` before exporter.export at :66 — a span exports exactly once even with explicit end() plus the withSpan finally end() at :128. index.ts has a single clean `export { Tracer }` (:26) with no duplicate re-export.
items.note: Idempotency is enforced at the span level via the `ended` flag; the withSpan finally cannot double-count. 12 observability tests pass.

items.name: ui-kit CSS injection / numeric prop coercion (packages/ui-kit, packages/ui/src/props.ts)
items.evidence: packages/ui-kit/src/components.tsx:323 `function numericProp(value, fallback) { return typeof value === "number" && Number.isFinite(value) ? value : fallback; }` used at Grid :105 (gridTemplateColumns repeat()) and Stack :77; packages/ui/src/props.ts:34 coerceNumber only converts when `value.trim() !== "" && Number.isFinite(n)`.
items.note: A CSS-bearing string or NaN/Infinity can no longer be interpolated into grid-template/gap — it collapses to the declared default. 142 ui-kit tests pass.

items.name: ui treeJsonSchema children allow-list (packages/ui/src/schema.ts)
items.evidence: packages/ui/src/schema.ts:106 emits `children: { type: "array", items: nodeRef }` (nodeRef = any node) whenever acceptsChildren is true, including a non-empty allow-list; validate.ts:31 `return policy.includes(childType)` DOES enforce the list. Test ui.test.tsx:292 asserts `list.properties.children?.items.$ref).toBe("#/$defs/node")` for `children: ["Text"]`, confirming the schema admits any child type.
items.note: Leaf-vs-container is mirrored exactly, but per-type allow-list is NOT encoded in the JSON Schema, so the model can emit a child the schema accepts yet validateTree later rejects. The schema comment claims it is 'as strict as the runtime policy' — true only for true/false policies, not lists. Tightening items to a oneOf of the allowed variants would close it; not a safety hole since the validator still rejects.

items.name: content-core paginate() page<=0
items.evidence: packages/content-core/src/query.ts:186-187 `validateRange(page, 1, Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER, "page"); validateRange(perPage, 1, ...)` throws a coded ValidationError before computing offset; validateRange defined at packages/content-shared/src/validation.ts:104.
items.note: page<1 (negative offset / wrong slice) and perPage<1 (Infinity/NaN totalPages) are rejected up front. Regression test query-pagination.test.ts exercises page 10 / out-of-range.

items.name: runtime getCollections() unpublished bypass
items.evidence: packages/content-core/src/runtime.ts:204-212 getCollections() now maps each name through `entries: getCollection(name)` (:210), and getCollection (:132-143) applies filterPublishedEntries when workflowConfig.filterUnpublished !== false. Comment at :206 states listing must not leak drafts that single-collection access hides.
items.note: The list path and single-collection path share the same publish/expiration filter, so getCollections no longer leaks unpublished/expired entries.

items.name: create-lesto workspace:* scaffold
items.evidence: packages/create-lesto/src/templates.ts:24 `const lestoDep = "latest";` with comment :20-22 'deliberately NOT the workspace:* protocol, which only resolves in-workspace'; all @lesto/* deps :43-49 use lestoDep, plus better-sqlite3/react/zod pinned. No `workspace:` occurs in any src file (only in explanatory comments).
items.note: A scaffolded app installs cleanly outside the monorepo because deps resolve to the `latest` dist-tag rather than the workspace protocol.

judge.rationale: Calibrating against the prior readiness run (run2 = 4.5; an earlier run hit 5.5 before regressing on tighter scrutiny). Real, committed progress lifts the floor: the crash-safety dimension is genuinely transformed (2->8) — the node web tier now has a per-request error boundary that never throws, a 1 MiB body cap, an async handler timeout, socket-level slow-loris limits, a process-level rejection net, graceful SIGTERM drain, and client-disconnect cancellation, all with green tests. Security primitives went from unwired IOUs to a real onion middleware pipeline plus a secureStack composition, with the four named bugs (CORS wildcard+credentials, CSRF session binding, webhook plaintext secret, webhook SSRF) genuinely fixed and the Cloudflare edge-bypass P0 closed. I verified independently that the create-lesto scaffold AND examples/estate DO mount secureStack({originCheck:{}}) (templates.ts:186, estate app.ts:38) — slightly better than the security assessor's 'scaffold ships none' claim — but it is still only originCheck, with no CORS, no rate-limit, and no CSRF token in any default deploy, and examples/blog mounts nothing. So the weighting works against a naive average: I confirmed the three heaviest dimensions still carry structural, not cosmetic, holes. Data-layer is the binding ceiling and is unmoved (5.5): I verified SqlStatement/SqlDatabase are fully synchronous (db/sql.ts:18-25 return values, not Promises) and there is zero Postgres adapter in any src file — only SQLite drivers and aspirational 'future Postgres' comments. A sync prepare/run/get/all surface cannot back an async Postgres pool without a breaking rewrite, and orm/connection.ts is still a module-global single connection with no pool or per-request scoping. That single fact caps the entire horizontal-scale story regardless of query-builder polish. Observability/deploy is 4: I confirmed @lesto/observability has zero importers (orphaned tracing core, in-memory exporter only), the readiness probe defaults to ()=>true with no caller wiring a real DB check (so /readyz lies), the edge has no access log, and lesto deploy's dynamic tier merely prints 'run lesto serve (dynamic)' (run.ts:370) with no atomic swap, health gate, canary, or rollback — it is a planner, not a deployer. RBAC is pure logic wired into zero request paths (no @lesto/rbac importer outside its own src). Net: this is production-ready for a single-instance SQLite node deploy behind a proxy, with correct, mountable security primitives — a genuine and defensible 5.5. It is NOT ready for a horizontally-scaled fleet (no Postgres, no pool, in-memory-only session/rate-limit stores), not safely deployable (file-copy deploy, no rollback), and not observable in production (no metrics/traces, lying readiness). Maturity (7) and framework correctness (8.5) are high but cannot pull the holistic score up past the structural data/deploy/observability floor on a weighted basis. 5.5 reflects a solid prototype that crossed the crash-safety and injection-safety thresholds but still rests on a single-process, undeployable, unobservable substrate.
biggestRemainingBlockers: No async data layer and no Postgres adapter: SqlStatement/SqlDatabase are 100% synchronous (packages/db/src/sql.ts:18-25) and the only concrete drivers are better-sqlite3/bun:sqlite. A real networked Postgres pool cannot back a sync prepare/run/get/all surface without a breaking rewrite. This caps horizontal scale entirely and is structural, not fruit.

No connection pooling or per-request scoping: orm/connection.ts uses a module-global active connection; @lesto/db's createDb takes one injected handle. No pool, no AsyncLocalStorage scoping — a fleet deploy has no safe concurrency story.

Deploy is a non-atomic file copy with no safety rails: lesto deploy's dynamic tier just prints 'run lesto serve (dynamic)' (run.ts:370); shipStatic copies files in place with no versioned dir, symlink swap, post-deploy health gate, canary, or rollback (no such tokens in packages/deploy/src).

Observability is effectively absent in the request path: @lesto/observability has zero importers, ships only an in-memory exporter (no OTel adapter), the kernel emits no spans/metrics, the edge has no access log, and the readiness probe defaults to ()=>true with no caller wiring a real dependency check, so /readyz lies about a dead DB.

Default-deploy security posture still thin: scaffold/estate mount only secureStack({originCheck:{}}) — no CORS, no rate-limit, no CSRF token; examples/blog mounts nothing; RBAC is pure logic wired into zero request paths; session and rate-limit stores are in-memory only (no SQL/Redis), so neither survives a restart or scales across a fleet.

Edge (Cloudflare) adapter lacks app-level body cap and handler timeout (fetch-handler.ts does await request.text() with no byte tally) — leans entirely on the Cloudflare platform; not parity with the hardened node tier.

Inherent sync-blocking DoS class: handlerTimeoutMs is a setTimeout and is explicitly not a defense against event-loop-blocking sync work (ReDoS, huge sync JSON.parse) — documented out of scope at server.ts:96-102.

lowHangingFruit.item: Wire a real readiness probe that checks the DB so /readyz stops lying
lowHangingFruit.why: isReady defaults to ()=>true and no caller passes a real probe, so /readyz returns 200 with a dead DB — directly defeats the health endpoint that load balancers and orchestrators trust for rolling restarts. A few-line change to have CLI runServe pass an isReady that pings the db handle.

lowHangingFruit.item: Add app-level body cap + handler timeout to the Cloudflare edge adapter for node parity
lowHangingFruit.why: The edge is the actual prod target per memory; decodeBody does await request.text() with no byte tally and there is no timeout — the one real DoS gap left after the node hardening. Mirror the node maxBytes tally and withTimeout race.

lowHangingFruit.item: Strengthen the default secureStack in the scaffold (add rateLimit + CSRF token, optional CORS)
lowHangingFruit.why: Scaffold/estate mount only originCheck; a default Lesto app has no rate-limit and no CSRF token. The primitives exist and compose in safe order in secureStack — flipping on rateLimit and csrf in templates.ts:186 hardens every new app's default posture with no architectural change.

lowHangingFruit.item: Add a request access log to the Cloudflare edge handler
lowHangingFruit.why: The edge wires logError (errors only) but has no per-request access log; the node tier already has the pattern (server.ts:845). Without it the real prod target is blind to traffic. Copy the node logRequest shape into toFetchHandler.

lowHangingFruit.item: Flip the CI format step from continue-on-error to blocking
lowHangingFruit.why: ws:format:check already exits 0 everywhere (content-* debt paid), so making the step blocking is a one-line flip that locks in the green state and prevents regressions at zero risk.

lowHangingFruit.item: git rm the 4 stray non-bun lockfiles
lowHangingFruit.why: Two package-lock.json (root + loom) and two pnpm-lock.yaml (content-markdown, content-shared) are tracked against a bun-only toolchain — pure hygiene that removes a footgun for contributors and CI.

lowHangingFruit.item: Wire @lesto/observability spans into the kernel/request path with the existing in-memory exporter
lowHangingFruit.why: The tracing core is built and idempotency-fixed but has zero importers. Starting a span per request in the dispatch path (even with the in-memory exporter) makes the request path instrumentable now and gives a single seam to later attach an OTel adapter — no architecture change to the tracer.

lowHangingFruit.item: Tighten ui treeJsonSchema to emit the allow-list child types instead of any-node
lowHangingFruit.why: The model-facing schema is looser than validateTree enforces (emits any node for a non-empty allow-list). Not a safety hole (validator still bites) but the documented last correctness polish gap; constrain items to the allowed $refs.

judge.keepGoingRecommendation: Pick the four high-value S-effort fruit first (real readiness probe, edge body-cap+timeout, stronger default secureStack, edge access log) — they are sub-day, no-architecture-change wins that close honest production gaps and will lift the score toward ~6.5. Then PIVOT to a structural phase. The three heaviest dimensions (data-layer, security-wiring depth, observability/deploy) are now gated by structural work that no amount of fruit can touch: the synchronous SQL surface + missing Postgres adapter + missing connection pool is one coherent rewrite (ADR-0004 must go async), and a real deploy path (atomic swap + health gate + rollback) plus an OTel exporter and persistent session/rate-limit stores are net-new substrate, not polish. Fruit gets you to a strong single-instance SQLite deploy; the fleet/observable/safely-deployable story requires the async-data + deploy-substrate phase.
